1: YHWH instructs Moses to command Israel about the offerings — 'My food for my food offerings, my pleasing aroma' — that must be presented at their appointed times. The festival-offering calendar (Numbers 28–29) is the most comprehensive such listing in the Torah. Each offering is described as ˀisheh YHWH (fire offering to YHWH) and reyach nihoah (pleasing aroma). The pleasing-aroma language runs from Noah's altar (Genesis 8:21) through the sacrificial system to Paul's application: 'Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Ephesians 5:2).
2: 'Command the people of Israel and say to them, My offering, my food for my food offerings, my pleasing aroma, you shall be careful to offer to me at its appointed time.' The word 'my food' (leḥmi) is striking: YHWH describes the offerings as his food. This anthropomorphism is not naive literalism but covenant-communion language: the offering-meal creates a table-sharing between YHWH and Israel. The Last Supper is the NT table-sharing between Christ and his people that the sacrificial meals enacted in type: 'This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood' (Luke 22:20).
3: The daily (tamid) offering: two one-year-old male lambs without blemish, each day. The word tamid (continual, regular, perpetual) describes the offering that never stops — morning and evening, day after day. Hebrews 7:27 contrasts this with Christ's sacrifice: unlike the Levitical high priests who offered daily, Christ offered himself 'once for all.' The contrast highlights both the typological character of the tamid (pointing to the ultimate offering) and its inadequacy (it must keep repeating).
4: One lamb in the morning and one at twilight — the dawn and dusk offerings bracket the entire day with sacrifice. The day begins and ends with the slaying of a lamb. The Passover lamb was slain 'at twilight' (ben haˁarbayim, between the two evenings); Christ was crucified at the hour of the afternoon tamid offering (the ninth hour, 3 p.m.) and died as the Passover lambs were being slaughtered (John 18:28; Numbers 19:14). The daily evening lamb-killing is the chronological type of the crucifixion hour.
5: The grain offering accompanying each lamb: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with a quarter hin of beaten oil. The grain offering (minḥah) accompanies the burnt lamb — never alone but always alongside the blood sacrifice. The combination of grain and blood in the daily offering creates the compound structure that the Lord's Supper embodies: bread and blood (body and cup). The physical materials of grain-and-oil point to the spiritual realities of sustenance and anointing that Christ provides.
6: The tamid is described as 'the burnt offering that was ordained at Mount Sinai' — a continuous burnt offering (olah tamid). The ordination at Sinai gives the daily offering its authoritative ground. The NT equivalent is Christ's once-for-all offering at Calvary, which is the ground of continuous priestly intercession: 'He holds his priesthood permanently... he always lives to make intercession for them' (Hebrews 7:24–25). The earthly tamid ordained at Sinai → the heavenly intercession ordained by the cross.
7: The drink offering accompanying the tamid: a quarter hin of strong drink per lamb, poured out in the sanctuary. The libation (nesech) of strong drink poured out before YHWH is an act of complete dedication — liquid poured out cannot be retrieved. Paul uses the libation image for his own martyrdom: 'I am already being poured out as a drink offering, and the time of my departure has come' (2 Timothy 4:6; compare Philippians 2:17). The tamid drink offering is the daily enacted theology of total self-offering.
8: The second lamb at twilight, with the same grain and drink offerings as the morning. The mirror-image evening offering completes the day's sacrifice frame. The symmetry of morning-and-evening sacrifice is the OT form of the principle that 'in him all things hold together' (Colossians 1:17) — the daily rhythms of time are sanctified by the bracket of sacrifice. The day begins in offering and ends in offering.
9: On the Sabbath: two additional one-year-old lambs without blemish, with double grain and drink offerings. The Sabbath receives a doubled offering — not the abolition of sacrifice but its intensification on the holy day. The doubled Sabbath offering reflects the principle that rest and worship are not opposed but complementary: the day of rest is also the day of heightened offering. Christ is the 'Lord of the Sabbath' (Matthew 12:8) and the fulfillment of its rest-and-worship theology.
10: The Sabbath burnt offering is in addition to the regular daily offering. The additive structure of the offering calendar — daily + Sabbath + monthly + annual — creates a layered sacrifice theology: each level adds to rather than replaces the previous. The cumulative structure of Israel's worship anticipates the NT principle that Christ's once-for-all sacrifice adds to (and fulfills) everything that preceded it without abolishing the theology it embodies.
11: At the beginning of each month (rosh ḥodesh): two young bulls, one ram, seven male lambs without blemish — the largest regular offering in the monthly cycle. The new moon offering is the largest regular non-festival sacrifice, reflecting the cosmic significance of the lunar cycle in Israel's calendar. The monthly offering frames time itself in sacrificial terms: even the marking of months belongs within the sacrificial structure of the covenant.
12: The grain offering accompanying the monthly bulls: three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour each. The grain accompaniments scale with the offering — bulls receive more grain than lambs. The proportionality of the offering system reflects the graduated character of covenant honor: 'To whom much is given, much will be required' (Luke 12:48). The offering calendar encodes proportionality as a theological principle.
13: The grain offering for the monthly ram and lambs specified in standard proportions. The careful enumeration of every accompanying offering — grain and drink with each animal — reflects the completeness and order that characterize Israel's worship. Paul's summary of the new covenant worship: 'I appeal to you therefore, brothers, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God, which is your spiritual worship' (Romans 12:1) — the entire person, not just certain parts, is the NT equivalent of this complete offering system.
14: The drink offerings: half a hin for a bull, a third for a ram, a quarter for a lamb. The libation scale mirrors the sacrifice scale. The drink offerings complete the full offering: blood (burnt animal), grain (food), wine (drink) — the three components of a meal. The Lord's Supper likewise consists of bread (grain) and wine (drink): the meal-structure of covenant communion runs from the Mosaic offerings to the NT table.
15: One male goat for a sin offering, in addition to the burnt offering and its drink offering. The sin offering (ḥattat) accompanies every major festival offering — atonement is built into every calendar occasion, not reserved for the Day of Atonement alone. The regularization of the sin offering throughout the year means that no gathering of Israel can occur without acknowledging the need for atonement. Christ's sacrifice is the once-for-all sin offering that underlies every gathering of his people: 'This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins' (Matthew 26:28).
16: The Passover is on the fourteenth of the first month. The Passover date establishes the anchor of Israel's liturgical year. The first month, fourteenth day — the full moon of the spring equinox — is the date of Israel's original liberation from Egypt (Exodus 12:1–14). Christ was crucified on the fourteenth of Nisan (John 18:28; Numbers 19:14), the Passover eve, at the hour of the Passover lamb's slaughter. The liturgical calendar's anchor and the gospel's central event are the same day.
17: The fifteenth day is the feast of unleavened bread (Ḥag Hamatzot) for seven days. The Passover lamb (14th) and the Unleavened Bread week (15th–21st) form a compound feast: liberation (Passover) followed by the purging of leaven (old life) from the community. Paul applies this directly: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed. Let us therefore celebrate the festival, not with the old leaven, the leaven of malice and evil, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth' (1 Corinthians 5:7–8). The feast's structure is the gospel's structure: atonement leads to new-life purification.
18: The first day of the Unleavened Bread feast is a holy convocation — no ordinary work. The sacred assembly on feast days is the OT form of the gathered church: the community ceases normal activity to focus on divine encounter. The NT equivalent: 'Not neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another' (Hebrews 10:25). The holy convocation encodes the principle that regular, deliberate gathering for worship is a covenant obligation.
19: The burnt offerings for the Passover feast: two young bulls, one ram, seven one-year-old lambs — the same as the new moon offering. The Passover week offering matches the monthly offering in scale, establishing Passover as a festival of maximum sacrifice intensity. The cross, as the fulfillment of Passover, is similarly the maximum sacrifice: 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all' (Romans 8:32) — the maximum divine self-giving enacted in human history.
20: The grain offerings accompanying the Passover burnt offerings in precise proportions. The feast offerings' careful enumeration communicates the principle that covenant worship is not improvised but revealed: YHWH specifies what pleases him, and Israel brings exactly that. The NT equivalent: Christ tells his disciples exactly how to remember his death (Luke 22:19: 'Do this in remembrance of me') — the Lord's Supper is revealed, not improvised worship.
21: Seven lambs through the seven days of Passover/Unleavened Bread. The seven-day duration of the feast creates a complete-week immersion in the liberation-and-purification theology. Seven days of unleavened bread — the old life's leaven entirely purged before the new week begins. The thoroughness of the purging is the thoroughness of the new creation: 'If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come' (2 Corinthians 5:17).
22: One goat for a sin offering — atonement accompanies the liberation feast. The sin offering placed within Passover week connects liberation (from Egypt/from sin) to atonement (the basis for liberation). The Passover lamb and the sin-offering goat together say: liberation is achieved through substitutionary death. Paul: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13) — redemption and atonement are inseparable.
23: These Passover offerings are in addition to the morning burnt offering. The layered offering system continues: the feast additions do not replace the regular daily offerings. The cumulative nature of the festival calendar — daily beneath everything, feasts added on top — mirrors the NT principle that Christ's unique sacrifice (the feast above all feasts) fulfills but does not abolish the underlying worship theology.
24: The same offerings each of the seven days of the Passover feast. The repetition through seven days creates a week of sustained worship immersion. The seven-day feast is the annual deep dive into the liberation-and-atonement theology that the daily offerings rehearse briefly each morning and evening. The sustained meditation on redemption across seven days finds its NT form in the sustained proclamation of the gospel as the church's permanent message: 'we preach Christ crucified' (1 Corinthians 1:23).
25: The seventh day of Unleavened Bread is another holy convocation — no ordinary work. The feast that begins with assembly (Numbers 28:18) ends with assembly (Numbers 28:25), framing the entire week within the gathered community. The bookend holy convocations mirror the new covenant's gathered-church structure: the church that assembles to proclaim the Lord's death until he comes (1 Corinthians 11:26) is bounded by its gatherings.
26: The Feast of Weeks (Shavuot): at the offering of new grain, after the seven weeks, a holy convocation and no regular work. The Feast of Weeks is calculated as seven weeks plus one day (fifty days) after Passover — hence the Greek name Pentecost (pentēkostē, fiftieth). Acts 2:1: 'When the day of Pentecost arrived...' — the Spirit's outpouring fulfills the feast of first-grain and first-fruits. The harvest festival of weeks becomes, in its NT fulfillment, the harvest of souls at Pentecost.
27: The Weeks/Pentecost offering: two young bulls, one ram, seven male lambs as a burnt offering — the same scale as Passover. The equivalence of the Passover and Weeks offerings places both at the maximum festival level. Passover is the Christological center (crucifixion); Pentecost is the pneumatological center (Spirit-outpouring). The equal sacrifice-scale of both feasts encodes the equal theological weight of the cross and the Spirit: both are central to the redemptive story.
28: The grain offerings accompanying the Weeks burnt offering. The pairing of grain (sustenance) with animal sacrifice (atonement) throughout the offering calendar creates a two-element theology of worship: the life offered (blood/animal) and the life sustained (grain/bread). Christ is both: 'I am the bread of life' (John 6:35) and 'the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world' (John 1:29).
29: The male goat sin offering for Pentecost. Atonement underlies every festival, including the joyful harvest feast. The sin offering at Pentecost encodes the principle that even the most joyful covenant moments require atonement as their foundation. The outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost does not bypass atonement but flows from it: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit' (Acts 2:38).
30: The Weeks offerings in addition to the regular daily offerings and their accompaniments. The additive structure persists to the last line of the feast description. The completeness of the offering calendar — nothing omitted, every type present at every festival — is the OT's fullest expression of the principle that 'every good gift and every perfect gift is from above' (James 1:17). The gifts flow from YHWH through the sacrificial system; the response is the full offering of the community.
31: All these offerings must be without blemish, with their grain and drink offerings. The closing requirement — 'without blemish' (temimim) — returns to the founding condition of every sacrifice. The unblemished requirement runs from the Passover lamb (Exodus 12:5) through the entire sacrificial system to Christ: 'like a lamb without blemish or spot' (1 Peter 1:19). The flaw in the animal type — no actual animal is morally unblemished — points to the necessity of the one truly unblemished sacrifice.
